L2_preconditions.label: Pressure/Haste/Misplaced Motivation
L2_preconditions.description: Brett was on his last day in Lauterbrunnen and described as 'desperate' to get one final jump before his flight home the next morning. This time pressure and misplaced motivation to complete the jump drove him to proceed despite clearly dangerous conditions and explicit warnings from companions.

L2_preconditions.label: Failure to perform pre-jump gear check
L2_preconditions.description: Neither the jumper nor his companion performed a pre-jump gear check at the exit point. The companion explicitly states that running a hand along the bridle routing would have immediately caught the misrouted bridle, which would have prevented the fatal jump.

L2_preconditions.label: Inattention during packing
L2_preconditions.description: The jumper packed in a rush the evening before because he wanted to go to sleep early to catch early morning conditions. This rushed, inattentive state during packing directly contributed to the bridle misrouting error, as the task of routing the bridle correctly requires careful attention to the deployment chain.

L2_preconditions.label: Spatial Disorientation
L2_preconditions.description: Complete flat light conditions — heavy fresh snow with no shadows, no contrast, and no texture on the snowfield — eliminated all visual depth cues, causing the jumper to misperceive his altitude and distance from terrain. The narrative states he clearly believed he had plenty of altitude and perceived himself to be much higher than he was, consistent with a visual illusion induced by featureless, low-contrast terrain.

L2_preconditions.label: Degraded visual conditions
L2_preconditions.description: Heavy fresh snow, low-level clouds, and complete flat light created a featureless white-on-white environment that physically removed the visual altitude references the jumper needed to monitor terrain proximity. This environmental condition directly degraded his ability to perceive altitude, enabling the attention failure in which he flew into terrain without any deployment attempt.

L2_preconditions.label: Overconfidence
L2_preconditions.description: The jumper chose to fly a new wingsuit line on an expedition in conditions of heavy fresh snow and complete flat light — conditions known to eliminate depth cues over snowfields. Despite 90 wingsuit BASE jumps and 5 BASE seasons providing sufficient knowledge to recognize flat light as a lethal hazard, he proceeded to fly proximity terrain without adopting compensatory measures such as conservative altitude margins, suggesting an unreasonable overestimation of his ability to maintain altitude awareness in these conditions.

L2_preconditions.label: Strong crosswind gust degraded exit stability
L2_preconditions.description: A strong crosswind gust caught the jumper immediately upon exit, pushing him sideways and back toward the wall and contributing to his transition from stable to head-low within the first second. The wind force actively degraded his ability to execute a stable exit and maintain forward separation from the cliff face.

L2_preconditions.label: Channelized attention on stability recovery
L2_preconditions.description: The narrative states the jumper never got a hand to his PC during three to four seconds of tumbling, indicating he failed to prioritize deployment over recovering stability. This fixation on regaining body position to the exclusion of the critical task of deploying the pilot chute directly enabled the failure-to-deploy error.

L2_preconditions.label: Substance Effects
L2_preconditions.description: Chris was out until 4am drinking heavily the night before, was clearly still impaired at the time of the jump with a splitting headache, vomiting twice on the trail, and visible unsteadiness. The narrative explicitly states the hangover affected his coordination, which directly degraded his physical ability to execute a stable exit.

L2_preconditions.label: Channelized Attention/Fixation
L2_preconditions.description: Chris fixated entirely on recovering stability during the approximately 6 seconds of freefall to the exclusion of monitoring altitude and deploying his pilot chute. The narrative states he spent the entire freefall trying to get back to belly rather than pitching his PC, demonstrating classic channelized attention on one task while neglecting the more critical task of deployment.

L2_preconditions.label: Allowing for progressively riskier behavior
L2_preconditions.description: The narrative states Chris had jumped hung over before without issues and that the decision to jump in this state was not unusual for him. This indicates a sustained pattern of jumping while impaired by alcohol that he was aware of and failed to correct, which enabled the degraded physical performance on this exit.

L2_preconditions.label: Insufficient tracking suit training
L2_preconditions.description: The jumper had only two skydives in the tracking suit and had never jumped it from a cliff. Accepted practice requires 40-50 tracking suit skydives before BASE use, and mastery of the suit's body position for maximum forward speed and glide. This massive training deficit directly produced his inability to achieve forward drive in the suit.

L2_preconditions.label: Insufficient BASE experience for slider-up cliffs
L2_preconditions.description: With 40 BASE jumps all from lower exits, the jumper had no experience with slider-up cliff jumping requiring sub-terminal tracking. The exit required mandatory tracking past a technical section where the wall comes back in, a task demanding sub-terminal tracking proficiency that his jump history had never developed.

L2_preconditions.label: Physical exhaustion from overnight drive
L2_preconditions.description: The jumper drove 14 hours straight through the night and was visibly exhausted at the exit. Physical exhaustion degrades coordination and motor performance, directly impairing his ability to execute the demanding body position control required to fly the tracking suit effectively.

L2_preconditions.label: Overconfidence from video observation
L2_preconditions.description: The jumper rejected a suggestion to start with a more forgiving exit, stating the chosen exit 'looked easy from the footage.' With zero cliff tracking suit experience and only two skydives in the suit, his assessment that the exit was easy reflects an unreasonable overestimation of his own capability relative to the specific task, beyond what a reasonable jumper in his position would conclude.

L2_preconditions.label: Failure to correct tracking suit proficiency gap
L2_preconditions.description: The jumper purchased the tracking suit a month before but completed only two skydives in it, far below the 40-50 tracking suit skydives accepted as minimum before BASE use. He was aware he owned new equipment requiring proficiency development and failed to undertake the necessary training over the available month before attempting a cliff jump requiring mandatory tracking.

L2_preconditions.label: Startle Response/Freeze
L2_preconditions.description: The sudden 180° off-heading opening directly facing the glass facade likely triggered a startle response, causing the jumper to grab the first toggle she could find rather than executing the trained two-riser or two-toggle arrest technique. The urgency of immediate object proximity degraded her ability to execute the correct sequence.

L2_preconditions.label: Insufficient object avoidance drill practice
L2_preconditions.description: With 90 BASE jumps over 3 seasons, the jumper's reaction to the 180° off-heading—grabbing a single toggle and pulling hard rather than using rear risers or both toggles to arrest forward speed—suggests insufficient practiced muscle memory for object avoidance corrections. Accepted practice emphasizes extensive repetition of heading correction drills at simple spans until the response is automatic, precisely so that under stress the correct technique is executed without conscious deliberation.

L2_preconditions.label: Overconfidence
L2_preconditions.description: The jumper stated he had done the exit dozens of times and 'knew the heading by feel,' expressing confidence that he could navigate by feel alone without visual references. This unreasonable overestimation of his ability to safely track through zero-visibility conditions—when all other jumpers declined to jump—directly drove his decision to exit.

L2_preconditions.label: Channelized Attention/Fixation
L2_preconditions.description: After reaching for the GoPro, the jumper appears to have become fixated on the camera adjustment to the exclusion of monitoring terrain proximity, body position, and separation from the wall. The ledge was in clear view for several seconds yet he made no corrective input, indicating his attention was channelized on the camera task rather than the critical flight parameters.

L2_preconditions.label: Distraction
L2_preconditions.description: The helmet-mounted GoPro presented an external stimulus that prompted the jumper to divert attention from the primary task of tracking away from terrain. The camera equipment created the opportunity for the mid-flight decision to adjust it, consistent with the domain reference noting that cameras are both physical snag hazards and psychological distractions.

L2_preconditions.label: Failure to correct recurring AoA control deficiency
L2_preconditions.description: The jumper experienced at least four near-miss stalls from the same AoA control problem over the preceding months. He explicitly acknowledged the deficiency and identified the corrective action (50 skydives working on stall recovery) but never followed through, continuing to jump cliffs every weekend. This sustained failure to correct a known recurring skill issue directly enabled the fatal technique error.

L2_preconditions.label: Pressure/Haste/Misplaced Motivation
L2_preconditions.description: The jumper knew he needed to pause cliff flying and return to skydiving for remedial training but kept jumping cliffs every weekend instead. The narrative describes a pattern of repeatedly putting off the acknowledged corrective action in favor of continued proximity flying, indicating a misplaced motivation to keep jumping that overrode his own rational assessment of the risk.

L2_preconditions.label: Startle Response/Freeze
L2_preconditions.description: Multiple witnesses reported the jumper 'completely froze the moment he saw the wires directly in front of him,' with arms remaining at his sides and no corrective action attempted during the approximately two seconds before impact. This automatic physiological freeze response directly prevented execution of the heading correction that the situation demanded.

L2_preconditions.label: Insufficient BASE Experience
L2_preconditions.description: The jumper had only 60 BASE jumps and just two prior antenna jumps, both from taller structures with guy wires further from the exit point. This limited antenna experience, particularly the lack of exposure to the tighter margins of shorter antennas with closer guy wires, likely contributed to his inability to execute an immediate heading correction under pressure. Practiced off-heading corrections from antennas with close guy wires develop the reflexive response needed to act within the narrow time window available.

L2_preconditions.label: Degraded visual conditions at night
L2_preconditions.description: The jump was conducted at night (23:30), which degraded the jumper's ability to visually acquire the guy wires and orient himself immediately after opening. Reduced visual conditions at night impair depth perception and spatial awareness, contributing to delayed recognition and response to the off-heading threat, compounding the freeze response.

L2_preconditions.label: Failure to correct known equipment deficiency
L2_preconditions.description: The jumper was explicitly aware of the damaged pilot chute — he examined the tear, was told by companions it needed replacement, and was offered spare PCs. Despite this known equipment deficiency, he failed to correct it by replacing the PC before the next jump, directly enabling his decision to jump with compromised gear.

L2_preconditions.label: Inadequate briefing or coordination
L2_preconditions.description: The narrative explicitly states 'Nobody really went through the exit order or separation plan properly. We just said who would go first second third. No specific delays were set and no one talked about flight paths or opening altitudes.' This absence of coordinated separation planning directly enabled both L1 errors: without agreed flight paths, David had no defined tracking direction to maintain separation, and without coordinated opening altitudes or delays, the second jumper's canopy was inflating in David's flight path at deployment.

L2_preconditions.label: Failure to correct progressively riskier behavior
L2_preconditions.description: The jumper had been progressively extending his delays from 4 seconds to 6 to 8 and sometimes longer over the past season. Other jumpers had spoken to him about it multiple times, yet he failed to correct this escalating pattern of increasingly dangerous low pulls.

L2_preconditions.label: Insufficient skydives in expert wingsuit
L2_preconditions.description: The jumper had only approximately 10 skydives in the Aura 1 expert wingsuit before taking it off a cliff, far below the accepted minimum of 40-60 skydives in a specific suit before wingsuit BASE. This insufficient familiarity with the suit's flight envelope meant he lacked the knowledge base to accurately assess whether he was ready to fly it in BASE, enabling the poor judgment decision.

L2_preconditions.label: Insufficient experience with expert suit flight characteristics
L2_preconditions.description: The narrative explicitly states the expert-level wingsuit has stall boundaries requiring many flights to understand and the jumper had not put in that time. With only ~10 skydives in the Aura 1 and prior experience limited to intermediate suits, he lacked the trained muscle memory and kinesthetic awareness needed to manage AoA and recognize the onset of stall in this suit, directly enabling the technique error mid-flight.

L2_preconditions.label: Thermal activity degrading flight conditions
L2_preconditions.description: The weather was described as sunny and hot with thermals producing. Active thermal conditions create variable lift and sink along a flight path, which can disrupt a wingsuit pilot's speed and AoA management. For a pilot unfamiliar with the suit's stall boundaries, thermal-induced turbulence or sink could have contributed to the AoA shift and loss of glide observed mid-flight.

L2_preconditions.label: Insufficient Skydiving Experience
L2_preconditions.description: The jumper had only 5 skydives total, far below the accepted minimum of 200+ skydives before beginning BASE. This extreme lack of foundational skydiving training meant the jumper lacked the body control, canopy skills, and deployment experience necessary to evaluate the risks of the jump, directly enabling the decision to attempt it.

L2_preconditions.label: Insufficient BASE Experience
L2_preconditions.description: The jumper had zero BASE jumps and attempted a slider-up tracking suit cliff jump — an advanced discipline requiring extensive BASE progression. With no BASE experience whatsoever, the jumper had no frame of reference for assessing the demands of the jump, enabling the grossly inappropriate decision.

L2_preconditions.label: Lack of BASE specific training
L2_preconditions.description: With only 5 skydives and zero BASE jumps, the jumper could not have completed the accepted progression path (200+ skydives, FJC, mentored progression from simple objects). The absence of any structured BASE training or mentorship enabled the decision to attempt an advanced cliff jump without understanding the risks involved.

L2_preconditions.label: Insufficient Skydiving Experience
L2_preconditions.description: With only 5 skydives, the jumper lacked fundamental freefall body control, tracking ability, deployment technique, and canopy piloting skills. Whatever the specific execution failure was — unstable exit, poor tracking, delayed deployment, or off-heading response — the near-total absence of skydiving experience meant the jumper had none of the foundational skills required to execute any phase of the jump.

L2_preconditions.label: Insufficient BASE Experience
L2_preconditions.description: Zero BASE jumps meant the jumper had never practiced any BASE-specific execution skill: exit technique from a cliff, sub-terminal tracking for object separation, altitude awareness in BASE context, or off-heading correction. This complete lack of BASE experience directly enabled whatever execution failure occurred during the jump.

L2_preconditions.label: Inattention during packing
L2_preconditions.description: Placing the pilot chute inside the main container instead of routing it to the BOC pouch is a gross deviation from the normal packing sequence that indicates a significant lapse in attention during packing. This fundamental misstep—packing the PC on top of the canopy under the closing flaps—suggests the jumper's attention was not adequately focused on the packing task.

L2_preconditions.label: Pressure/haste from deteriorating weather
L2_preconditions.description: The narrative describes deteriorating weather with increasing wind and rain throughout the afternoon, and the jumper was the last one at the exit going back and forth on whether to jump or hike down. This suggests the jumper was packing and preparing under time pressure from worsening conditions, which likely contributed to the rushed or distracted state that produced the packing error.

L2_preconditions.label: Failure to perform pre-jump gear check
L2_preconditions.description: A standard pre-jump gear check verifying that the PC is properly routed to the BOC pouch would have caught this error before exit. The fact that the jumper exited with the PC trapped inside the container indicates that no adequate gear check was performed, or that it failed to include verification of PC routing.
